learning_objectives: Identify amplitude, frequency, period, angular frequency, and phase in a sinusoid.
Connect a sinusoid to a rotating phasor intuition.
Convert a cosine-sine combination with the same frequency into one phase-shifted cosine.

visual_plan.rationale: The textbook page has no usable figure, and sinusoids are best learned by seeing how amplitude, frequency, period, and phase change the waveform. A static sine-wave reference is less valuable than a controlled React + Canvas demo because the key student confusion is parameter change.
visual_plan.cram: Use the demo to recognize amplitude, period, and phase-shift patterns quickly.
visual_plan.standard: Use the demo to connect each formula to one representative waveform and one phasor projection.
visual_plan.top_score: Use the demo to expose sign conventions, phase direction, and same-frequency compression traps.

blocks.instruction: Render Page 1 as a minimal overview only. Include exactly two short sections: 'Section Objective' and 'Concepts In This Section'. Under Section Objective, write one sentence: 'Learn how to read, sketch, and combine sinusoidal signals used throughout signals and systems.' Under Concepts In This Section, list only these concept names as bullets with no explanations: sinusoid model; amplitude; frequency and period; angular frequency; phase; phasor view; converting cosine-sine sums into one sinusoid.

blocks.explanation_instruction: Start Page 2 with heading '## 1. The basic sinusoid model'. Explain in 100–140 words that this is the standard real sinusoidal signal. Define every symbol: \(x(t)\) is the signal value at time \(t\), \(C\) is amplitude, \(f_0\) is frequency in cycles per second, and \(\theta\) is phase in radians. State when to use it: whenever a waveform repeats smoothly like a cosine. Exam trigger: a phrase like 'sinusoid', 'frequency', 'phase', or 'periodic cosine'. Common misuse: treating \(\theta\) as degrees unless the problem explicitly says degrees. Include one minimal example: for \(x(t)=3\cos(2\pi\cdot 5t+\pi/4)\), amplitude is 3, frequency is 5 Hz, and phase is \(\pi/4\).

mode_specific_visual_use.cram: Let students drag sliders and memorize what each parameter visually changes.
mode_specific_visual_use.standard: Use one default example and ask students to observe amplitude, period, and phase directly.
mode_specific_visual_use.top_score: Add sign-sensitive phase observations and compare positive versus negative phase.

blocks.title: Interactive sinusoid: amplitude, frequency, period, and phase
blocks.demo_instruction: Build a React + Canvas demo showing \(x(t)=C\cos(2\pi f_0t+\theta)\). Use a clean white background, navy waveform, muted teal period marker, and muted red phase marker. Include sliders for \(C\) from 0.5 to 4, \(f_0\) from 0.25 to 5 Hz, and \(\theta\) from \(-\pi\) to \(\pi\). Show the current formula numerically above the canvas. Draw a horizontal time axis, vertical amplitude axis, a double-arrow marking one period \(T_0\), and a faint reference cosine with \(\theta=0\). Include a small optional phasor inset: a rotating vector whose horizontal projection matches the waveform. Default settings: \(C=2\), \(f_0=1\), \(\theta=\pi/3\).
student_tasks: Increase \(C\) and observe that peaks get taller but spacing does not change.
Increase \(f_0\) and observe that more cycles fit into the same time window.
Change \(\theta\) and observe that the waveform shifts horizontally without changing height or frequency.
Use the period marker to verify that doubling \(f_0\) halves \(T_0\).
blocks.fallback: If interactive rendering fails, use a simple Canvas-generated static comparison with three separate rows: amplitude change, frequency change, and phase change. Do not use GPTImage2.

blocks.explanation_instruction: Start Page 3 with heading '## 2. Period and frequency are reciprocals'. Explain in 100–130 words that \(T_0\) is the time for one full repeat and \(f_0\) is the number of repeats per second. Connect this directly to the OCR logic: cosine repeats when its angle changes by \(2\pi\), so \(2\pi f_0t+\theta\) needs a time change of \(1/f_0\) to complete one cycle. Use case: convert between seconds per cycle and cycles per second. Exam trigger: a problem gives one of \(T_0\) or \(f_0\) and asks for the other. Common misuse: saying larger frequency means larger period; it is the opposite. Example: if \(f_0=50\) Hz, then \(T_0=1/50=0.02\) s.

Continue Page 3 under the period formula. Explain in 80–110 words that \(\omega_0\) is angular frequency in radians per second, while \(f_0\) is ordinary frequency in cycles per second. The factor \(2\pi\) appears because one cycle equals \(2\pi\) radians. State the equivalent relation \(\omega_0=2\pi/T_0\) in inline math only. Exam trigger: the sinusoid is written as \(\cos(\omega_0t+\theta)\) instead of \(\cos(2\pi f_0t+\theta)\). Common misuse: forgetting the \(2\pi\) and treating Hz as rad/s. Example: if \(f_0=10\) Hz, then \(\omega_0=20\pi\) rad/s.

Start Page 4 with heading '## 3. Phasor view: one rotating vector creates the sinusoid'. Explain in 110–150 words for students with mixed complex-number background. Say that \(X\) is a complex number called a phasor; its magnitude gives amplitude and its angle gives phase. The real-part operator \(\Re\{\cdot\}\) means we take the horizontal projection of the rotating vector. Use the demo's phasor inset as the visual anchor: as the vector rotates at angular speed \(\omega_0\), its horizontal projection traces the cosine wave. Use case: same-frequency sinusoidal terms can be combined by combining their phasor components. Exam trigger: a problem asks to combine sinusoids or interpret phase. Common misuse: forgetting the real part and treating the complex exponential itself as the final real signal.

Start Page 5 with heading '## 4. Convert a cosine-sine sum into one sinusoid'. Explain in 130–160 words that this works only because both terms have the same angular frequency \(\omega\). State that the target is a single cosine with amplitude \(R\) and phase \(\theta\). In inline math, give the coefficient-matching rules: \(A=R\cos\theta\), \(B=-R\sin\theta\), so \(R=\sqrt{A^2+B^2}\) and \(\theta=\operatorname{atan2}(-B,A)\). Exam trigger: terms like \(\cos(\omega t)+k\sin(\omega t)\) or \(a\cos(\omega t)-b\sin(\omega t)\). Common misuse: giving only amplitude \(R\) and forgetting the final phase-shifted sinusoid. Mention that phase sign is the main trap because expanding \(\cos(\omega t+\theta)\) produces a minus sign on the sine coefficient.

blocks.instruction: Continue Page 5 with one representative worked example and one quick exam note. Work this example step by step in 110–150 words: convert \(x(t)=\cos(\omega t)+2\sin(\omega t)\) into one cosine. Show that \(A=1\), \(B=2\), \(R=\sqrt{1^2+2^2}=\sqrt{5}\), and \(\theta=\operatorname{atan2}(-2,1)\approx -1.107\) rad. Final answer must be \(x(t)=\sqrt{5}\cos(\omega t-1.107)\). Then add one short near-miss: for \(3\cos(\omega t)-4\sin(\omega t)\), the sine coefficient is \(B=-4\), so \(\theta=\operatorname{atan2}(4,3)\), not \(\operatorname{atan2}(-4,3)\). End with the exam note: always write the final single sinusoid, not just \(R\) and \(\theta\).

Create the recap page titled '📌 Key Takeaways'. Use 5 concise bullets. Include these formulas explicitly as display or inline math, not only prose: \(x(t)=C\cos(2\pi f_0t+\theta)\) (B.13); \(T_0=1/f_0\); \(\omega_0=2\pi f_0=2\pi/T_0\); \(x(t)=\Re\{Xe^{j\omega_0t}\}\); \(A\cos(\omega t)+B\sin(\omega t)=R\cos(\omega t+\theta)\) with \(R=\sqrt{A^2+B^2}\) and \(\theta=\operatorname{atan2}(-B,A)\). Keep each bullet under 25 words. End with one bridge sentence: 'Next, these sinusoid ideas become easier to manipulate using complex numbers and phasors.'

knowledge_points.label: Reading amplitude, frequency, and phase from the sinusoid model

questions.stem: For \(x(t)=4\cos(2\pi\cdot 7t-\pi/6)\), which parameter reading is correct?
questions.explanation: The model is \(C\cos(2\pi f_0t+\theta)\), so \(C=4\), \(f_0=7\) Hz, and \(\theta=-\pi/6\) radians.

wrong_option_explanations.B: The amplitude is the multiplier outside the cosine, not the frequency.
wrong_option_explanations.C: \(14\pi\) is the angular frequency in rad/s, not the hertzian frequency.
wrong_option_explanations.D: Frequency in this form is in Hz, and phase is in radians unless degrees are stated.

knowledge_points.label: Converting among period, frequency, and angular frequency

questions.stem: A sinusoid has period \(T_0=0.01\) s. What are \(f_0\) and \(\omega_0\)?

wrong_option_explanations.B: This treats period as frequency instead of taking the reciprocal.
wrong_option_explanations.D: The factor \(2\pi\) belongs in angular frequency, not hertzian frequency.

questions.hint: First take the reciprocal, then multiply by \(2\pi\).

questions.stem: In the interactive demo, doubling \(f_0\) while keeping \(C\) and \(\theta\) fixed causes what visual change?
options: A. The waveform has twice as many cycles in the same time window, and the period halves.
B. The waveform becomes twice as tall, and the period stays fixed.
C. The waveform shifts right by half a period, but spacing stays fixed.
D. The waveform changes from cosine to sine.
questions.explanation: Frequency counts cycles per second. Doubling frequency compresses the waveform horizontally, so \(T_0=1/f_0\) halves.

wrong_option_explanations.B: Height is controlled by amplitude \(C\), not frequency.
wrong_option_explanations.C: Horizontal phase shift is controlled by \(\theta\), not frequency alone.
wrong_option_explanations.D: Changing frequency does not change the signal type from cosine to sine.

questions.hint: Look at the period marker when \(f_0\) changes.

questions.stem: What does \(x(t)=\Re\{Xe^{j\omega_0t}\}\) mean in the phasor view?
options: A. The real signal is the horizontal projection of a rotating complex vector.
B. The signal is complex-valued and should keep both real and imaginary parts.
C. The phasor removes the frequency from the signal permanently.
D. The phase must always be zero if the signal is real.
questions.explanation: The rotating complex vector carries amplitude and phase; taking \(\Re\{\cdot\}\) gives the real cosine waveform.

wrong_option_explanations.B: The real-part operator explicitly extracts a real signal.
wrong_option_explanations.C: The factor \(e^{j\omega_0t}\) still contains the frequency dependence.
wrong_option_explanations.D: A real sinusoid can have any phase.

knowledge_points.label: Combining same-frequency cosine and sine terms into one sinusoid

questions.stem: Convert \(x(t)=\cos(\omega t)+2\sin(\omega t)\) into one cosine.

wrong_option_explanations.B: The phase sign is wrong because the sine coefficient equals \(-R\sin\theta\).
wrong_option_explanations.D: The requested form is one cosine; switching to sine changes the phase convention.

questions.stem: For \(x(t)=3\cos(\omega t)-4\sin(\omega t)\), which phase setup is correct for \(R\cos(\omega t+\theta)\)?

wrong_option_explanations.C: This uses \(B\) directly and misses the minus sign in \(B=-R\sin\theta\).
wrong_option_explanations.D: This does not match the coefficient rules.

questions.stem: A classmate converts \(2\cos(\omega t)+2\sin(\omega t)\) and reports only \(R=2\sqrt{2}\). Explain why this answer is incomplete.
questions.ideal_answer: The amplitude is correct, but the problem asks for one sinusoid, so the phase must also be included. Since \(A=2\) and \(B=2\), \(\theta=\operatorname{atan2}(-2,2)=-\pi/4\), so the final form is \(2\sqrt{2}\cos(\omega t-\pi/4)\).
grading_rubric: Must state that amplitude alone is incomplete.
Must give or describe the final single-sinusoid form.
Must use the correct negative phase sign for the positive sine coefficient.
questions.explanation: This catches the common exam trap of stopping after magnitude and forgetting the final phase-adjusted signal.
questions.hint: The final answer must look like \(R\cos(\omega t+\theta)\), not just \(R\).

section_specific_rules:
section_specific_rules:
- This section is computationally central and may receive strong exam-priority weighting.
- Favor worked examples, conversion fluency, trap warnings, and quiz alignment.

exam_priority_guidance.markdown:
# Background HW1 Distillation — Section Filtered

Current section: B.2
Relevant topic buckets: sinusoid_phasor
Preview-only topic buckets: complex_algebra

## Relevant Concepts
- Phasor interpretation of sinusoidal terms
- Converting a cosine-sine combination into a single sinusoid

## Relevant Problem Types
- Combine sinusoidal terms into one sinusoid

## Required Example Patterns
- cos(ωt) + k sin(ωt) to one sinusoid
- a cos(ωt) - b sin(ωt) to one sinusoid with phase explanation

## Common Traps
- Confusing radians and informal degree intuition
- Getting phase sign wrong in sinusoid compression
- Giving amplitude without final phase-adjusted sinusoid

## Hard Rule
- Section identity wins. Do not import formulas, examples, quiz bias, or Key Takeaways from non-relevant topic buckets.
- Preview-only topics are limited to one short bridge sentence and must not become a teaching page.

topic_filter.rule: Only relevant topics may shape lesson structure, examples, quizzes, and Key Takeaways. Preview-only topics may receive at most one short bridge sentence and must not be listed as core formulas.

a cos(ωt) - b sin(ωt) to one sinusoid with phase explanation
common_traps: Confusing radians and informal degree intuition
